1: Isaiah 21:1 opens the first of three oracles in this chapter: 'The oracle of the Desert of the Sea.' The 'Desert of the Sea' (massa midbar-hayam) is an unusual designation — no desert-sea exists. Most interpreters identify this as Babylon, approached from the desert to the east and surrounded by the marshy Tigris-Euphrates flood plain (called a 'sea' in Babylonian texts). The opening image: 'Like whirlwinds sweeping through the Negeb, it comes from the desert, from a dreadful land' — the sirocco whirlwinds (suphoth, whirlwinds/storm-winds) that sweep through the Negev with devastating speed and heat. The oracle comes from a 'dreadful land' (erets nora'ah, a fearful/terrible land) — Babylon is both the source of terror and the target of divine judgment.
2: 'A grim vision has been shown to me: the traitor betrays, the plunderer plunders. March, O Elam! Besiege, O Media! All the groaning Babylon caused I will bring to an end' — the vision's content: 'marchasheh badoghed boged, vehashoded shed, a hard vision is shown to me; the treacherous dealer deals treacherously, and the plunderer plunders.' The poetic structure: two pairs of repeating root words (boged/badoghed; shoded/shed) emphasize the self-fulfilling nature of violence. 'March, O Elam! Besiege, O Media!' (ali Eilam, tsuri Madai, go up O Elam, lay siege O Media) — Elam (the region of southwestern Persia, Khuzestan) and Media (the Median empire of northwestern Iran) are explicitly named as the conquerors of Babylon. The historical fulfillment: Cyrus the Great combined Persian and Median forces to conquer Babylon in October 539 BCE.
3: 'Therefore my body is racked with anguish; labor pains have seized me like those of a woman giving birth; I am too shaken to hear, too shattered to see' — the prophet's extraordinary personal anguish at the vision. 'Al-ken maleu chaluim motnai, therefore my loins are filled with anguish' (chaluim, writhing/pain). 'Tziroth achazatuni ketziroth yoledet, labor pains have seized me like the pains of a woman giving birth.' The prophetic anguish over the fall of Babylon is not triumphalism but genuine suffering — the prophet identifies with the victims even when the perpetrator-city deserves judgment. 'I am too disturbed to hear, too dismayed to see' (naaweiti mishome'a nivchalti mirroth, I was bent from hearing, I was terrified from seeing) — the vision is too overwhelming for normal prophetic reception.
4: 'My mind reels; a shuddering terror grips me; the evening I had longed for has been turned into dread' — continued personal agony: 'ta'ah levavi, my heart staggers/is bewildered' (ta'ah, to stray/wander = the heart losing its orientation). 'A shuddering terror has seized me' (balahah biathatni, a shuddering/appalling thing terrifies me). 'The evening I had longed for has been turned into dread' (neshef chesheqti sam li lacharedah, the twilight I desired has been turned for me into trembling). The 'evening I had longed for' may refer to the watchman's hope for the morning (Isaiah 21:8) or to the banquet scene of Isaiah 21:5 — the festive evening of Belshazzar's feast (Dan 5) turned into sudden terror. The prophetic experience is not detached observation but visceral identification.
5: 'Set the table, spread the rugs, eat and drink! Rise up, O princes, and oil the shields' — the festive scene interrupted by military alarm. The commands: 'aroch hashulchan, spread/set the table; tsafo hatsephiyyah, watch/lay out the watch; achal shato, eat, drink.' This is the banquet scene — Belshazzar's feast in Dan 5 describes exactly this: Babylon's rulers eating and drinking from the vessels of YHWH's temple when the writing on the wall appears. The sudden interruption: 'qumu hasarim mushu magen, rise O princes, oil/anoint the shields!' — the military command to prepare for battle (anointing shields with oil kept the leather supple for combat). The transition from festive table to military alarm is the moment Babylon's doom arrives.
6: 'For this is what the Lord said to me: Go, station a watchman; let him report what he sees' — the watchman commission: 'lekh amod hatsopeh asher yir'eh yagid, go, station the watcher; what he sees let him declare.' The prophet is commanded to post a sentinel (tsopeh, the watchman/lookout) on the city wall or watchtower. The watchman motif is developed most fully in Ezekiel (Ezekiel 3:17; Isaiah 33:1-9: 'Son of man, I have made you a watchman for Israel') where the prophet is made the community's sentinel responsible for warning of approaching danger. The 'what he sees let him report' = the watchman's function is to observe and faithfully transmit — accurate reporting without embellishment or suppression.
7: 'The watchman saw a column of cavalry — riders in pairs, a caravan of donkeys, a caravan of camels — and he listened intently, with close attention' — the watchman's observation: a large military force approaching, described as cavalry in pairs (rekev tsemed parashim, a chariot/pair of horsemen), donkey caravan (chamor), camel caravan (gamal). The mixed cavalry-and-transport force suggests both the military strike force and the supply train of the invading army. 'He listened intently with close attention' (vayiqqeshev qeshev rabbah, and he listened with great attention) — the watchman is completely alert, every sense focused. The combination of sight (saw the column) and hearing (listened intently) describes total watchman vigilance.
8: 'Then the lookout called out: My lord, I stand on the watchtower continually by day, and I keep my post every night' — the watchman's report of his own faithfulness: 'anokhi omed al-hammitzpeh tamid yomam, I stand on the watchtower continually by day, ve'al mishmarti anokhi nitsav kol-halayelot, and at my guard post I station myself all the nights.' The watchman is not asleep at his post — he has maintained continuous vigilance. This self-report establishes the credibility of what follows: when the watchman reports, he speaks from exhaustive, unbroken observation. Habakkuk 2:1 uses the same language: 'I will stand at my watch and station myself on the ramparts; I will look to see what he will say to me.'
9: 'Here comes a column of men, horsemen in pairs! Then the watcher answered: Fallen, fallen is Babylon! All the carved images of her gods are shattered to the ground!' — the watchman's climactic report: 'nafala nafala bavel, fallen, fallen is Babylon!' The doubled verb (nafala nafala, fallen-fallen) is the emphatic completion — total, irreversible fall. 'All the carved images of her gods he has shattered to the ground' (vekhol-psilei eloheiha shibar la'arets, and all the carved images of her gods he has broken to the ground). The specific destruction of Babylon's divine images is both historical (Cyrus's forces would have dismantled the cult objects) and theological (the idols that 13:2 said would be shattered are shattered). Revelation 14:8 quotes this directly: 'Fallen! Fallen is Babylon the Great!' Revelation 18:2 expands it for the eschatological Babylon.
10: 'O my people, crushed and winnowed — what I have heard from the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, I now declare to you' — the pastoral address to the exiles: 'Crushing and son of my threshing floor' (midushati uvenmad-goren, my crushed one and son of my threshing floor) — the exiles described as grain that has been crushed and winnowed, subjected to the threshing of exile. The 'my people' address (the prophet addressing the exiles as YHWH's) reestablishes the covenant relationship even in the context of judgment. What the prophet received as a grim vision (Isaiah 21:2) he now declares for the exiles' benefit: 'what I have heard from YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, I have declared to you.' The oracle of Babylon's fall is good news for those under Babylon's power.
11: 'The oracle of Dumah. A voice calls to me from Seir: Watchman, what is left of the night? Watchman, what is left of the night?' — the Dumah/Edom oracle: the shortest of Isaiah's burden oracles. 'Dumah' (dumah = silence/stillness) is likely a wordplay on 'Edom' (edom, the nation south of the Dead Sea, traditionally descended from Esau). Seir (the mountain range = Edom's territory, Genesis 36:8-9). The voice calling from Seir: 'shomer meh-milleil shomer meh-milleil, watchman, what of the night? watchman, what of the night?' The double question creates urgency: the Edomites are in darkness and desperately want to know if morning (relief) is coming. The 'night' is both literal and metaphorical — the darkness of their situation under Assyrian pressure and the theological darkness of not knowing YHWH's purpose.
12: 'The watchman said, Morning is coming, but so is the night. If you would inquire, inquire; come back again' — the watchman's cryptic response: 'ata boqer vegam-layelah, morning comes and also night.' Both morning (relief/salvation) and night (further darkness/judgment) are coming — the answer is deliberately ambiguous. The watchman is not refusing to answer but giving an honest, nuanced answer: some morning is coming, but more night follows. The closing invitation: 'im-tivayun bayu, if you will inquire, inquire; return, come.' Return (shuv, turn/repent — the covenant vocabulary) and come. The oracle offers conditional openness: if Edom turns (to YHWH?), the conversation can continue. The ambiguity of the Edom oracle reflects the ambiguity of Edom's covenantal status — related to Israel (Esau-Jacob) but outside the covenant.
13: 'The oracle concerning Arabia. You caravans of the Dedanites, take shelter in the thickets of Arabia' — the Arabia oracle: Dedan (modern al-Ula/Mada'in Saleh in northwestern Saudi Arabia, a major North Arabian trading center on the incense route) is commanded to take shelter (linu, lodge/spend the night) in the thickets/forest of Arabia (bya'ar ba'arav, in the thicket of Arabia). The Arabic 'Arabia' (arav = wilderness/steppe) is both a geographic designation and a description of the terrain. Dedan was a major waystation on the incense route from South Arabia (Yemen) to the Mediterranean. The Dedanite caravans that normally passed through openly are now sheltering in brush cover — fleeing military threat.
14: 'Bring water to the thirsty, O people of Tema; go out with food to meet the fugitives' — Tema (modern Tayma, an oasis city in northwestern Saudi Arabia, mentioned in Nabataean and Neo-Babylonian inscriptions — Nabonidus, the last Babylonian king, actually resided in Tema for ten years) is called to bring water (mayim, water) to the thirsty (tzame, the thirsty ones) and 'with food they went out to meet the fugitives' (belihem qidmu noddedim, with their food they went out to meet the wandering refugees). The humanitarian action — providing water and food to fleeing refugees — is commanded in the context of the military rout. The Arabian tribes who normally traded peacefully are now hosting war-refugees.
15: 'For they have fled from the swords, from the drawn sword, from the bent bow, and from the fury of battle' — the triple instrument of flight: 'mi-penei cheravot nasu, from before the swords they fled' — mepenei cherev hantuyyah (from before the outstretched/drawn sword), umepenei qeshet derukhah (from before the bent/strung bow), umepenei kovad milchamah (from before the heaviness/fury of battle). The three weapons and the 'fury of battle' specify the military context — this is not natural disaster or famine but military campaign. The Assyrian campaigns into Arabia (particularly Esarhaddon and Ashurbanipal who campaigned against Arab tribes) are the historical background for Arabia's destabilization.
16: 'For this is what the Lord said to me: Within a year, reckoned as a hired worker counts a year, all the wealth of Kedar will come to nothing' — Kedar (the dominant North Arabian tribal confederation, associated with the descendants of Ishmael, Genesis 25:13; mentioned in Assyrian records as Qidri) will be destroyed 'within a year' (od shanah kishne sakir, within a year as the years of a hired worker — the same precise time formula as 16:14 for Moab, indicating exactly one contractual year). 'All the wealth of Kedar will come to nothing' (kol kevod Qedar, all the glory/wealth of Kedar). Kedar was famous for its herds (Psalms 120:5; Song of Solomon 1:5; Isaiah 60:7 mentions Kedar's rams as tribute), its archers (Isaiah 21:17), and its trading capacity.
17: 'The remaining archers — the warriors of Kedar — will be greatly diminished, for the LORD, the God of Israel, has spoken' — the remnant of Kedar's warriors reduced: 'veshe'ar mispar qeshet gibborei benei Qedar yim'atu, and the remnant of the number of bows of the mighty warriors of the sons of Kedar will be few.' Kedar's military identity was its archers — the Arabian bow warriors were renowned in the ANE. The diminishment of the bows = the diminishment of Kedar's military capacity. The closing authorization: 'ki YHWH Elohei Yisrael dibber, for YHWH the God of Israel has spoken' — the God of Israel speaks the fate of Arabia. YHWH as God of Israel is sovereign over the Arabian desert tribes who had no connection to the Sinai covenant.
